stem: [[n]] [[noun]] each own one distinct [[item]]. The [[n]] [[item]]s are collected and then handed back, one to each person at random. In how many ways can the [[item]]s be returned so that no person receives their own [[item]]?
solution: The number of derangements is \( D_{[[n]]} = [[n]]! \sum_{k=0}^{[[n]]} \frac{(-1)^k}{k!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts all [[n]]! permutations, ignoring the no-fixed-point restriction.
This removes only the single identity permutation, not every arrangement with a fixed point.
This is \( ([[n]]-1)! \), a common wrong guess for derangements.
This halves the total permutations, which is not how fixed points are excluded.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: [[n]] [[noun]] each own one distinct [[item]]. The [[n]] [[item]]s are collected and handed back at random, one to each person. What is the probability that NO person receives their own [[item]] back? Give your answer as a fraction.
solution: The probability of a derangement is \( \dfrac{D_{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{[[n]]} \dfrac{(-1)^k}{k!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability of the ONE fully-fixed permutation, not the derangement probability.
This is the naive guess that only one person keeps their own item, \( \frac{n-1}{n} \).
This is the probability a single specific person keeps their own item.
This is the complement: the probability that AT LEAST ONE person gets their own item back.
This adjusts the correct probability by an unrelated fraction.
This adjusts the correct probability by an unrelated fraction.

stem: [[n]] [[noun]] each own one distinct [[item]]. The [[n]] [[item]]s are handed back at random, one to each person. In how many ways can the [[item]]s be returned so that EXACTLY [[m]] people receive their own [[item]] back (and everyone else receives someone else's)?
solution: Choose which [[m]] people keep their own [[item]] in \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[m]]} \) ways, then derange the remaining \( [[n]]-[[m]] \) items: \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[m]]} D_{[[n]]-[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This lets the remaining people be arranged in ANY way, not just deranged (no further fixed points).
This is an unrelated ratio of factorials, not the partial-derangement count.
This only chooses who keeps their own item and forgets to arrange the rest at all.
This deranges the rest but forgets to choose which [[m]] people keep their own item.
This is off from the correct count by [[m]].
This is off from the correct count by [[m]].
